summary: I traced 9 end-to-end chains across the Dashboard, Analytics, Security Center, and Scanning screens (Flutter) into the orbguard.lab Go backend. WHAT WORKS: `/api/v1/stats` is genuinely wired to the indicators/sources/campaigns repositories and returns real DB-backed counts; getStats() -> ThreatStats powers most of the Analytics 'Threats' and 'Protection' tabs (provider.stats is real). `/api/v1/stats/protection` is partially real (critical/high DB counts drive the score). Health/Ready checks are real. The native device-scan path in main.dart `_performScan` invokes real platform method channels. WHAT IS INCOMPLETE/FAKE: (1) The dashboard-summary chain is broken by a contract mismatch — the backend `/stats/dashboard` handler returns a totally different JSON shape than DashboardSummary.fromJson requires, so getDashboardSummary() throws on every call (error swallowed), leaving the Dashboard Protection card, Threat overview, Device Health card, and Recent Alerts permanently empty. (2) The dashboard 'Scan' button passes onScan: () async => [] — a hardcoded placeholder, so dashboard scans never run. (3) scanning_screen progress/items-scanned is faked with Random() and time-based animation. (4) analytics_service.go is almost entirely hardcoded/simulated (trends, distributions, top indicators/domains/IPs, MITRE, geo, alert/detection metrics, source health) with explicit 'in production, query database' comments and a placeholder 98.5% detection rate. (5) Analytics field-name mismatches mean even the simulated data mostly doesn't render. (6) Protection feature flags are hardcoded in stats.go. (7) Reports persistence is in-memory only and generation is a 2s sleep stub with a 404 download URL. (8) Several dead UI controls.

The Scam Detection feature is partially real but undermined by an unconfigured backend service and three concrete client/server contract mismatches that force the app onto its on-device fallback for nearly every path.

WHAT WORKS: The UI (scam_detection_screen.dart) is fully wired for Text/URL/Phone — real text inputs, an Analyze button that calls the provider, a result card, a History tab (in-memory), and a Patterns tab. The provider methods (analyzeText/analyzeUrl/checkPhone/reportScam) call real api_client methods that hit real backend routes registered in router.go. The backend ScamDetector.Analyze + intent_classifier.go (rule-based) + entity_extractor.go (regex) produce genuine, non-mocked text/URL analysis even without an LLM. speech_analyzer.go is a real Whisper+LLM implementation (only analyzeVoiceCharacteristics is a stub, and it is gated off). Report endpoints persist into in-memory pattern/phone DBs.

WHAT IS INCOMPLETE/BROKEN: (1) The backend ScamDetector is constructed with an EMPTY config (ai.ScamDetectorConfig{}) in cmd/api/main.go:218, so EnableLLM/EnablePatternDB/EnablePhoneRep/EnableVision/EnableSpeech are ALL false — no LLM, no pattern DB matching, and phone/image/voice analyzers return errors. (2) The result's Confidence field is never set (only RiskScore), so the app's confidence bar is always 0%. (3) GET /scam/patterns returns a 'scam_types' object but the app reads 'patterns' → always empty → app silently uses hardcoded default patterns with fake detection counts. (4) The phone-reputation client path (/scam/phone/reputation?number=) does not match the backend route (/scam/phone/{number}), and even if it did the phone analyzer is disabled — so phone checks always return 'clean/safe'. (5) The indicators field is []ScamIndicator (objects) on the server but parsed as List<String> on the client, throwing a cast error that drops the ENTIRE server response and silently falls back to local on-device analysis. Net effect: most 'AI' results the user sees are actually the Flutter local heuristic, not the backend.

domain: SMS Protection (OrbGuard anti-spyware client + OrbGuard Lab threat-intel backend)
summary: The SMS Protection domain is largely a facade. The backend SMS analyzer (LAB sms_analyzer.go) is genuinely implemented — real URL/sender/intent/pattern analysis with threat-intel DB lookups — and routes are registered. The Flutter UI (inbox/check/stats/detail screens) is fully built and the manual "Check" tab can call the API. HOWEVER the end-to-end chain is broken in several critical ways: (1) The Flutter request/response contract does NOT match the backend's JSON at all — the client sends 'content' but the backend reads 'body', and the client parses fields (risk_score, extracted_urls, detected_intents, matched_patterns, recommendation, should_block, threat levels safe/suspicious/dangerous) that the backend never emits (it emits confidence, urls, intent_analysis, pattern_matches, recommendations[], no should_block, and levels safe/low/medium/high/critical). So every real API analysis silently decodes to an empty/SAFE result. (2) The inbox is permanently empty: SmsProvider.loadMessages() is a TODO no-op, and SmsPlatformService (which actually reads the device inbox via a wired native channel) is orphaned — never instantiated or attached to the provider. (3) Blocked-sender and stats persistence are TODO stubs, so blocks and stats reset every launch. (4) The /sms/report-false-positive route is aliased to GetStats — false-positive reports are discarded. (5) GetStats returns hardcoded zeros. (6) SMSScannerService and the on-device SmsClassifier ML are dead code never invoked by this domain. (7) The Stats screen shows hardcoded 'Active/Enabled/Connected' protection statuses. Net: the only path that can show a real (non-empty) result to a user is the manual Check tab, and even that is corrupted by the contract mismatch.

The URL Protection chain is partially real but has serious gaps. WHAT WORKS: The primary "Check URL" flow is wired end-to-end — UI (url_protection_screen.dart) -> UrlProvider.checkUrl -> OrbGuardApiClient.checkUrl (POST /api/v1/url/check) -> URLHandler.CheckURL -> URLReputationService.CheckURL, which performs real heuristic analysis (threat-intel DB lookup via repos.Indicators, phishing pattern regexes, typosquatting, suspicious TLD/IP/homograph characteristics, Redis caching). The route is registered and the request payload {url} matches. WHAT IS BROKEN: (1) A response CONTRACT MISMATCH silently drops most of the verdict — the Go URLCheckResponse returns `category` (singular), `threat_level`, `description`/`block_reason` with NO `categories` array, `severity`, `risk_score`, `threats`, or `recommendation` fields, but the Dart UrlReputationResult.fromJson reads exactly those missing keys, so severity always defaults to 'info', riskScore to 0.0, and categories/threats to empty. This in turn makes the UI's stats counters (phishing/malware/scam blocked) always read zero and the risk display wrong. (2) Google Safe Browsing is wired as `nil` in cmd/api/main.go:137, so the real GoogleSafeBrowsingClient is never invoked despite existing. (3) Whitelist/blacklist and remove are in-memory only on the backend (no DB persistence; RemoveFromList is an explicit no-op) AND the provider never calls the backend whitelist/blacklist endpoints at all — lists live only in Dart memory and the persistence methods are TODO stubs, so all custom lists are lost on app restart. (4) Two entire Flutter services (SafeBrowsingService, PhishTankService) and the on-device UrlClassifier ML model are dead/placeholder code never used in the URL chain. (5) Domain-details (GET /url/reputation/{domain}) returns 404 for essentially all real domains because WHOIS/SSL/DNS enrichment is unimplemented, while the Dart DomainReputation model expects rich WHOIS data.

domain: Dark Web / Digital Footprint / Identity / Privacy
summary:
Traced all four feature chains (Dark Web breach monitoring, Digital Footprint / data-broker removal, Identity Protection, Privacy Protection) UI->provider->api_client->route->handler->service->datasource.

WHAT WORKS (real): Dark-web email/password breach checks are genuinely wired — Flutter checkEmail/checkPassword -> /api/v1/darkweb/check/email|password -> DarkWebHandler -> DarkWebMonitor -> HIBPClient, which makes real HIBP v3 / pwnedpasswords (k-anonymity) HTTP calls. The Go HIBPClient is production-grade (real severity calc, recommendations, graceful 404/429/401 handling, simulated fallback only when no API key). Privacy event recording, audit, and tracker domain-block lookups have real handlers/services. The Go RemovalService genuinely generates CCPA/GDPR/opt-out letters and can POST opt-out forms; the digital_footprint Scanner orchestrates real concurrent sub-scanners.

WHAT IS INCOMPLETE/FAKE: (1) The entire Identity Protection feature is a local in-memory simulation — credit scores are NEVER populated (updateCreditScore has zero callers), credit freeze/unfreeze/recovery-cases only mutate RAM and contact no bureau, and asset scanning (_checkDarkWeb/_checkDataBrokers/_checkPublicRecords) all `return []`. (2) Digital Footprint has a hard contract break: Flutter calls /api/footprint/* (missing /v1) AND never sets include_data_brokers/include_dark_web AND sends address as string vs backend's addresses[] — so every real call fails or returns empty and the provider silently substitutes hardcoded fake results (12 brokers, privacy score 45) plus a fake 3s progress animation. (3) getBreachAlerts contract mismatch: Flutter reads response.data['alerts'] but backend returns {unread,read} → runtime crash, so alerts never load. (4) Dark-web monitored assets/alerts and footprint removal requests are in-memory only (explicit "would be database in production" / "until DB repo is available"), keyed off an unauthenticated user_id="default". (5) Hardcoded production API keys (HIBP/LeakCheck/IntelX) committed in client source. (6) darkweb_provider load/save persistence are empty TODOs. (7) RequestRemoval handler never calls ProcessRequest, so no opt-out form is ever actually submitted — requests sit at "pending" forever.

The QR Security feature has a genuinely implemented backend: service.AnalyzeQRCode (LAB internal/domain/services/qr_security.go) does real content-type parsing (URL/email/phone/SMS/WiFi/crypto/vCard/event/app-link) and real heuristic threat analysis (URL shorteners, suspicious TLDs, IP-as-host, typosquatting, premium-rate numbers, open/WEP WiFi, invalid crypto addresses), plus a live URL-reputation lookup. The route POST /api/v1/qr/scan -> QRSecurityHandler.Scan is correctly wired (router.go:490, handlers.go:119, cmd/api/main.go:183/263). The Flutter chain (qr_scanner_screen -> QrProvider.scanQrCode -> OrbGuardApiClient.scanQrCode) reaches it. HOWEVER the feature is functionally broken end-to-end by a JSON CONTRACT MISMATCH between the Go response model and the Dart parser: the most important field, threat_level, uses an enum (safe/low/medium/high/critical) that the Dart SmsThreatLevel enum (safe/suspicious/dangerous/critical) cannot parse, so every 'low'/'medium'/'high' result silently falls back to SAFE — a phishing/typosquatting QR the backend flags as 'high' is shown to the user as safe. Several other response fields (content vs raw_content, risk_score vs threat_score, recommendation vs recommendations[], analyzed_at vs scanned_at) never bind, so the result card renders empty content, a permanent 0% risk bar, and no recommendations. Additionally: history persistence is stubbed (TODOs), 'Report False Positive' and 'Report submitted' are dead no-op UI with no endpoint, and the backend's /qr/preview and /qr/check-url routes have no Flutter client method. The backend threat engine is real; the client just can't see its verdicts.

The ML Analysis screen (ml_analysis_screen.dart) renders three tabs (Models/Anomalies/Insights) sourced entirely from four backend calls: getMLModels (GET /ml/models), getAnomalies (GET /ml/anomalies), getMLInsights (GET /ml/insights), runMLAnalysis (POST /ml/analyze). The backend ML algorithms themselves (IsolationForest, KMeans, RandomForest, FeatureExtractor, EntityExtractor) ARE genuinely implemented in pure Go — real tree-building, path-length anomaly scoring, k-means clustering, RF classification, Shannon-entropy feature extraction (the math/rand use is legitimate for isolation-forest splits, not fake inference). HOWEVER the end-to-end chain is broken in several critical ways: (1) the models are NEVER trained — mlService is constructed in cmd/api/main.go:171 with AutoTrain:true but there is NO auto-train goroutine/scheduler, so IsTrained() is always false and EnrichIndicator returns nil AnomalyScore/Cluster/Severity; (2) three of the four screen endpoints are wrong-contract aliases that all return GetStats JSON (no anomalies/insights arrays exist), so the Anomalies and Insights tabs are permanently empty and Run Analysis adds nothing; (3) the entire on-device TFLite ML stack (TFLiteMLService, MlService, BehaviorAnalyzer, sms/url classifiers) is dead code — no tflite dependency, no asset models, no native channel handler, and not referenced by any screen, while falsely reporting isModelLoaded=true. The screen's Models tab will show real (but untrained) model metadata; everything else is non-functional. 7 chains traced.

The BACKEND is largely real: the GraphRepository (LAB internal/infrastructure/graph/repository.go) runs genuine Neo4j Cypher for GetCorrelation/FindRelated/SharedInfrastructure/DetectCampaigns/Traverse/GetStats, and the CorrelationEngine (LAB internal/domain/services/correlation_service.go) implements real temporal/infrastructure/TTP/network/campaign correlation. However the FRONTEND Threat Graph and Correlation screens are effectively dead: every data source they call points at endpoints that DO NOT EXIST on the backend router, so both screens always hit the catch block and render empty. Specifically, ThreatGraphScreen loads from GET /graph/nodes and GET /graph/relations (no such routes; router only has /graph/correlation/{id}, /related/{id}, /traverse, /search, /stats, etc.), and CorrelationScreen loads from GET /correlation and POST /correlation/run (no such routes; router only exposes POST /correlation/ for Correlate, /batch, /analyze, GET /correlation/stats, etc.). The same broken methods are reused by threat_hunting_provider.dart, so threat hunting graph/correlation features are also non-functional. The graph search dialog is a dead control (sets a local string, never calls the real /graph/search route). One real backend placeholder remains: GraphService.CalculateTTPSimilarity returns hardcoded zeros. I traced 6 chains end-to-end (graph nodes, graph relations, graph related, correlation results, run correlation, ttp-similarity).

domain: Threat Hunting / Actors / Campaigns / Intelligence / Sources / MITRE
summary:
MITRE is the one fully-real chain: mitre_screen -> MitreProvider -> getMitreTactics/getMitreTechniques -> /mitre/tactics & /mitre/techniques -> MITREHandler -> MITREService.LoadEmbeddedData() (real embedded mobile ATT&CK tactics/techniques with correct {"tactics":[...]} / {"techniques":[...]} envelopes the client expects). The Intelligence Core screen (indicators list/check/batch-check) is also genuinely wired to a DB-backed repository.

Everything else in this domain is broken by response-envelope contract mismatches or is stubbed:
- listCampaigns()/listActors() use PaginatedResponse.fromJson which reads json['items'], but campaigns.go and actors.go return {"data":[...],"total":N} (key "data"). So the Campaigns and Threat Actors screens ALWAYS render empty regardless of real DB/default data the backend returns. Same class of bug for getIntelSources() (reads ['sources'] vs backend ['data']) -> Intelligence Sources screen permanently shows the empty state.
- Threat Hunting screen's Graph / Correlation / ML tabs call provider.loadGraphData/loadCorrelationRules/loadMLModels which hit endpoints that either don't exist (/graph/nodes, /graph/relations — no such routes) or have wrong method/shape (GET /correlation has no handler; GET /ml/models -> ML.GetStats returns a stats object, not {"models":[...]}). All errors are swallowed in the provider, so these tabs are permanently empty/dead.
- The core Threat Hunting findings engine (threat_hunting_service.dart) returns real results only for permission-based and hidden-app rules (Android MethodChannel). The hash, domain, network, and path rule checks all return empty lists with "would compare/check..." comments — so IOC sweep, C2 domain, data-exfil network, and malware-hash hunts produce no findings even on a compromised device. No backend threat-hunting endpoint exists; hunts are purely on-device.
- Investigation cases are stored only in an in-memory map (ThreatHuntingService._cases) with no persistence and no backend route — lost on app restart.
- Intelligence Sources Add-Source dialog and per-source enable/disable Switch are pure local no-ops (backend has only GET /sources, no create/update). Backend intelligence.go has TODOs: Report does not persist (only logs), ListMobile/MobileSync ignore platform filter, ListCommunity is hardcoded empty, and MobileSync always returns empty indicator arrays.

domain: Device Security / Permissions / Access Setup (OrbGuard APP + LAB)
summary: The Permissions / Elevated-Access / Root-Jailbreak chains are largely REAL: the Flutter PermissionManager/SpecialPermissionsManager and ElevatedAccessSetupScreen call a native MethodChannel ('com.orb.guard/system') that is genuinely implemented in Android MainActivity.kt (checkRootAccess, checkUsageStatsPermission, checkAccessibilityPermission, openUsageStatsSettings, requestStoragePermission, checkAccessLevel, getSetupInstructions) and iOS AppDelegate.swift (checkRootAccess->checkJailbreak). These produce real values. However the Device Security (anti-theft) chain is fundamentally broken: EVERY app endpoint path in orbguard_api_client.dart mismatches the backend chi routes registered in router.go. The app calls flat paths (/device/security/status, /device/locate, /device/command/{cmd}, /device/status/lost|stolen|recovered, /device/anti-theft/settings, /device/sim/history, /device/sim/trusted, /device/security/audit-os, /device/location/history) but the backend only registers parameterized routes (/device/{device_id}/security-status, /device/{device_id}/locate, /device/{device_id}/command, /device/{device_id}/mark-lost, etc.). Result: every DeviceSecurityProvider call returns 404, every error is silently swallowed, and the Device Security screen permanently shows default fake data (Device Secure, score 100, empty SIM/location/vulnerability lists). Additionally the backend service persists nothing meaningful to a DB (in-memory maps for locations/SIMs/commands/selfies/settings), there is no on-device code that actually reports location/SIM/selfie data or polls pending commands, and several advanced_detection_modules native methods are unimplemented on both platforms. Two instruction screens (root/jailbreak) have dead buttons. Auth device-register also returns api_key while client reads token.

What works: the backend NetworkSecurityService has real, functional analysis logic (WiFi audit heuristics, evil-twin/rogue-AP detection, ARP-spoof checks, real TLS/SSL probing via tls.Dial, DNS provider lookup), and the three screens are fully built UIs wired to providers. What is incomplete: almost every end-to-end chain in this domain is broken at one or more hops. (1) No native platform code exists for ANY of the three WiFi/firewall method channels used by this domain (com.orb.guard/wifi, com.orbvpn.orbguard/firewall, com.orbguard/rogue_ap) — every scan/monitor call throws MissingPluginException which is silently swallowed, so WiFi scanning, firewall connection monitoring, and rogue-AP scanning all produce permanently empty results while the UI looks alive. (2) The app's WiFi-audit request/response models do not match the backend's models at all (flat vs nested request; completely different result fields), so even the audit calls that do reach the backend audit an empty request and parse a default-empty result. (3) Six endpoints the app calls (/network/threats, /vpn/status, /vpn/connect, /vpn/disconnect, /dns/status, /dns/enable, /dns/disable) have no backend route — all 404, errors swallowed. (4) The rogue-AP REST routes that do exist are admitted placeholders ('map to wifi/audit for now') aliased to wrong handlers, and DELETE trusted has no route, so the trusted-AP list is a client-side illusion with zero persistence. (5) The Network Firewall feature is a complete facade: no event source, no enforcement, in-memory rules lost on restart, and the toggle shows 'Firewall Active' even when native enable failed. (6) RogueAPDetectionService and ZeroDayProtectionService are dead code never instantiated by any provider/screen. (7) Backend placeholders: GetStats returns zeroed placeholder stats, ConfigureDNS/UpdateVPNConfig persist nothing, DNS hijack/leak checks are simplified stubs run on the server's own resolver, and the gateway-MAC store is an unscoped in-memory map that will cross-contaminate users.

What works: the App Security screen UI itself (tabs, filters, sorting, detail sheet) is fully built and wired to AppSecurityProvider; the backend AppAnalyzer performs real permission/privacy/security scoring and a real threat-intel DB lookup (indicators by package name and APK hash); GET /apps/trackers and /apps/permissions/dangerous serve real static data; Android's MainActivity has a real native getInstalledApps() implementation. What is broken/incomplete: the entire App Security chain is non-functional end-to-end — the app loads its app list from GET /api/v1/apps/installed which does not exist in the backend (and conceptually should come from the device's native channel, which exists but is unused), the analyze request/response contracts between Flutter and Go are mutually incompatible (permissions as strings vs objects → guaranteed 400; flat result fields vs nested backend JSON → all detail fields parse to empty/defaults), and the risk-score scale (0-100 backend vs 0-1 thresholds in the provider) is wrong. The Supply Chain feature is equally dead: its MethodChannel 'com.orbguard/supply_chain' has no native implementation on any platform, and all three /supply-chain/* backend endpoints it depends on don't exist, with every failure silently swallowed so the UI shows "Scan complete" with zero results. AppVettingService (simulated permissions/trackers, no manifest parsing) and AppUpdateMonitorService (no native channel, hardcoded scores) are dead code referenced by nothing. Backend stubs: GetAppReputation returns hardcoded "safe", GetStats returns empty structs, ReportApp only logs, tracker detection matches tracker SDK prefixes against the app's own package name (never matches real apps), and analysis results are never persisted.

What WORKS: the Flutter-side native persistence scanning is genuinely real and production-grade — DesktopSecurityProvider dispatches to three platform scanner services (macos/windows/linux_persistence_scanner_service.dart, ~4,300 lines total) that run real OS commands via dart:io Process.run (launchctl/osascript/plutil/codesign/crontab on macOS; reg query/schtasks/powershell Get-AuthenticodeSignature on Windows; systemctl/crontab/atq/stat/semodule on Linux). No platform MethodChannels are needed or used — Process.run works on desktop. Quarantine/restore/delete (move file + launchctl unload, reg delete, schtasks /Change, sc config, systemctl disable) and the metadata-driven restore are real, as are result caching and JSON export. What is BROKEN/FAKE: the entire backend half of the feature is dead. (1) The five desktop_security services (PersistenceScanner, CodeVerifier, NetworkMonitor, BrowserScanner, VTClient) are never constructed in cmd/api/main.go, so all 18 /api/v1/desktop/* routes return 503 in production. (2) The screen's initial _loadData calls three endpoints (GET /desktop/persistence, /desktop/apps, /desktop/firewall) that do not exist in the backend router at all — the screen shows an error card on every launch and the Code Signing tab can never populate. (3) Even if wired, the backend scanners scan the SERVER host (runtime.GOOS, server lsof/netstat, server browser profiles), not the user's machine — the Network and Browser tabs present server-side data as the user's device data. (4) JSON field contracts mismatch (is_suspicious/remote_ip vs risk_level/remote_address), so data would render as all-Safe/blank anyway. (5) The Firewall tab is largely cosmetic: hardcoded "Active and protecting" status with a no-op switch, quick actions that show a fake success snackbar, Add Rule that only appends to a local list, and a backend rule store that is in-memory only and never applies anything to any OS firewall.

The forensics domain has a substantive backend (LAB internal/forensics: real shutdown.log parser, DataUsage.sqlite parser, sysdiagnose archive extractor, logcat parser, IOC pattern matching) but the screen-to-backend wiring is almost entirely broken. The Flutter API client hand-builds URLs that diverge from both api_config.dart and the registered routes, so 4 of 9 endpoints 404 (shutdown-log, full-analysis, quick-check, IOC stats). Request body keys mismatch on every analyze endpoint (content/path vs log_data/backup_path/archive_path/db_path), so logcat 400s and backup/sysdiagnose/data-usage hit the server with empty paths and 500. The response parser expects a 'findings' array that the backend never emits (it emits 'anomalies'/'detected_threats'), so even a successful call would render 'No Threats Found'. The IOCs tab permanently displays hardcoded fake stats (2500 IOCs) because the stats call always fails and the catch block substitutes invented numbers. Backup/sysdiagnose analysis is architecturally impossible as built: the user types a device-local path that the remote server stats on its own filesystem; no upload endpoint exists for those artifacts. On the backend, hash IOC matching uses admitted placeholder hashes, the IOC scanner is never wired to the threat-intel DB (LoadFromIndicators has zero callers), and the backup parser's bplist metadata parser is an empty stub. The client-side IOSForensicService is a complete hollow shell (every analyzer returns an empty list with 'in production this would...' comments) and is dead code, never imported. Net effect: no forensic flow in the app currently produces a real result end-to-end.

What works: the LAB backend has real, well-structured handlers and delivery mechanics for webhooks (HMAC signing, retry workers, test/rotate-secret), integrations (real Slack/Teams/PagerDuty HTTP delivery), SIEM forwarding (real Splunk HEC/Elastic bulk/Sentinel HTTP senders), and STIX/TAXII conversion that reads real indicators from the DB. The APP executive-protection heuristic engine (homoglyph/lookalike/urgency analysis) is genuinely implemented on-device. What is incomplete: almost every screen-to-backend chain in this domain is broken or fake. The Enterprise Overview and Compliance screens call endpoints that do not exist (/enterprise/events, /enterprise/devices, /enterprise/compliance/controls, /enterprise/compliance/reports/generate), so both screens always show the error state. The entire enterprise policy/BYOD feature is an in-memory Dart mock that never talks to the backend (and silently reports devices as compliant on failure). The SIEM screen's add/remove/test connection are local-only fakes and its three list calls parse JSON keys the backend never returns, so lists are always empty. The STIX/TAXII screen is dead in production config (taxii_server.enabled: false), parses a nonexistent 'servers' key, and hits a trailing-slash 404 on collections. Webhooks and Integrations list parsing mismatches the backend's bare-array responses, and the 'enabled'/'is_connected' field contracts don't match. On the backend, MDM sync/alerts are explicit simulations, zero-trust posture and all compliance assessments return hardcoded/pseudo-derived scores presented as real, and every enterprise store (MDM, policies, SIEM configs, compliance reports/findings, webhooks, integrations) is in-memory only and lost on restart.

domain: OrbNet VPN / Social Media / Playbooks / Settings / YARA
summary: What works: the LAB backend has a genuinely functional OrbNet DNS-filtering core (ShouldBlockDomain with exact/wildcard/regex rules loaded from real threat-intel indicators, Redis caching, emergency block, sync), a real YARA scan engine (compiled rules, severity/risk scoring, APK/IPA scan endpoints), and a real playbook trigger/condition/worker engine. The APP's SettingsProvider genuinely persists to SharedPreferences, and the YARA rules list chain (screen -> getYaraRules -> GET /yara/rules -> YARAService.GetRules) is the only fully wired end-to-end chain in this domain. What is incomplete/fake: (1) The OrbNet VPN screen's connect button is a 2-second timer that flips a bool — no VPN is ever established; its 3 data calls hit alias routes whose response shapes/fields don't match what the app parses (rules vs domains key, dashboard-stats keys, server flag/latency fields), and /vpn/connect|disconnect|status + /dns/status called by network_provider have no backend routes at all. (2) Social Media Monitoring is 100% client-side simulation: hardcoded privacy scores, a fabricated impersonation alert for any username >5 chars, invented data exposures, a report button that only debugPrints, and no backend or persistence. (3) Playbooks: all 18 backend action handlers are log-and-return-true stubs, playbooks/executions live only in memory, and the list/executions API responses are bare arrays the app cannot parse (it reads ['playbooks']/['executions']), so the screen can never show real data; Create Playbook dialog is dead. (4) The YARA screen's scan feature fabricates Mimikatz/CobaltStrike detections via Future.delayed instead of calling the real /yara/scan backend; upload dialog is dead; ParseRule always returns valid and SubmitRule discards submissions (handler TODOs). (5) Settings persist but are consumed by nothing — protection toggles, biometric lock, VPN auto-connect/kill switch, quiet hours, and custom API server/key have zero functional effect (setBaseUrl is never invoked); Export Data and Delete All Data only show snackbars. (6) Admin report-review endpoints are TODO stubs that return success without touching the database. OrbNet servers/rules and playbooks have no DB persistence, so all API-created state is lost on backend restart.

domain: API contract cross-check: APP (orbguard Flutter) ↔ LAB (orbguard.lab Go backend)
summary: Exhaustively cross-checked all ~145 endpoint calls in APP lib/services/api/orbguard_api_client.dart + api_config.dart against all routes registered in LAB internal/api/router.go (+ scam_detection.go RegisterRoutes). WORKS (path+method+handler match, real logic behind them): SMS analyze/batch/patterns, URL check/batch/reputation, QR scan, darkweb email/password checks + alerts, apps analyze/trackers, network wifi audit + dns/check, YARA scan/rules, MITRE tactics/techniques, campaigns/actors, orbnet dns-block/batch/sync, stats + stats/dashboard + stats/protection, alerts read/clear, analytics suite (threats/alerts/detections/sources/geo/dashboard/reports), desktop network/codesign/VirusTotal/full-scan endpoints, webhooks/playbooks/integrations basics, graph/related/{id}, WebSocket /ws/threats. BROKEN: the entire auth chain is non-functional-by-design (backend login accepts any password, middleware accepts any non-empty bearer token, and the app never stores a token because it reads 'token' where the backend returns 'api_key', so every protected call 401s on a missing Authorization header); all 12 device-security client methods, 4 forensics methods, privacy clipboard, and scam phone-reputation use wrong paths/methods (client hardcodes literals instead of the correct ApiEndpoints constants); the footprint feature calls /api/footprint/* (missing /v1) so data-broker removal is 100% dead; POST /indicators/check is aliased to a GET-style handler that ignores the JSON body; supply-chain, VPN connect/disconnect/status, DNS enable/disable/status, BYOD, policy-assignment, enterprise events/devices/controls, indicators/{id}, apps/installed, network/threats, graph nodes/relations, desktop persistence/apps/firewall GETs, and correlation list/run have NO backend routes; the rogue-AP, SIEM, and ML anomalies/insights routes are fake aliases returning wrong-shaped or unrelated data that the app parses into permanently-empty lists. Roughly 60 backend routes are never called by the app (admin, MDM, zero-trust, most graph/correlation/yara/mitre/privacy/qr/orbnet management surface).

domain: gRPC threat-intel server (LAB internal/grpc/threatintel + proto/threatintel/v1, wiring in cmd/api/main.go, consumers in orbnet/orbmesh/APP)
summary: The gRPC server IS started in cmd/api/main.go (listener on cfg.Server.GRPCPort=9002, grpc.NewServer, threatIntelServer.Register, health service, Serve at main.go:299-318), and — contrary to the audit premise — server.go contains REAL implementations of all 9 RPC methods that hit the actual data sources (repos.Indicators.CheckBatch/GetByValue/List/Upsert, repos.Sources.GetStats, Redis cache, streaming.EventBus). However, the entire service is FUNCTIONALLY DEAD over the wire: types.go is hand-written placeholder code instead of protoc-generated output, and its RegisterThreatIntelligenceServiceServer (types.go:32-35) is an EMPTY no-op with the literal comment "For now, this is a placeholder". No grpc.ServiceDesc is ever registered, the hand-written message structs do not implement proto.Message (no marshaling), so no client on any language can invoke a single RPC — every call to threatintel.v1.ThreatIntelligenceService returns "unknown service" / Unimplemented. The port-9002 server effectively serves ONLY the grpc health check, which falsely reports threatintel.v1.ThreatIntelligenceService as SERVING. The Makefile has a working `proto` target (line 57-62) generating into proto/threatintel/v1 (package threatintelv1), but it has never been run (zero *.pb.go files in the repo) and the hand-written types diverge from the .proto contract (ThreatUpdate is completely different shapes; StreamThreatsRequest field types differ). Nothing consumes the service: zero references to threatintel/ThreatIntelligence in orbnet, orbmesh, or the Flutter app (no Dart gRPC at all), despite the .proto comments claiming "used by OrbNet for DNS filtering" and "for OrbMesh DNS filtering". Additionally GetCampaign/ListCampaigns ignore the fully-built CampaignRepository (repos.Campaigns) and serve only hardcoded models.DefaultCampaigns(). What works: the 9 method bodies themselves are substantive and DB-backed and would work once exposed via generated stubs. What is incomplete: protoc generation never run, registration no-op, proto/type contract drift, no consumers wired, health service lies about serving status.

The auth layer is almost entirely decorative and production-unsafe. LAB's APIKeyAuth middleware accepts ANY non-empty bearer token (the validation branch is an empty if-block), so every "protected" endpoint is effectively open. Login/Refresh issue cryptographically-random tokens but (a) never validate the supplied password against any user store, and (b) the issued tokens are stored in Redis yet NEVER checked by the auth middleware, so the entire token lifecycle is theater. Admin auth falls back to a hardcoded "dev-admin-token". On the APP side, device registration and token refresh both read the wrong JSON keys from the backend response (contract mismatches), so the client never actually stores an access token from registration and refresh is permanently broken. Three real third-party breach-API keys are hardcoded into the Flutter source. Deployment manifests ship hardcoded JWT secrets and an "secret123" API key. CORS is configured with allow_credentials=true alongside localhost origins. What DOES work: token generation uses crypto/rand, Redis-backed rate limiting is real and wired, device persistence to Postgres is real, and config secret loading via env vars is correctly plumbed (the YAML defaults are empty). The core problem is that authentication is unenforced and the client/server auth contracts don't match.

domain: Build / Test Baseline (APP: orbguard Flutter, LAB: orbguard.lab Go)
summary: WHAT WORKS: LAB compiles cleanly (`go build ./...` exit 0) and `go vet ./...` is clean. APP analyzes in ~10s with no compile-blocking issues except one analyzer-level error. WHAT IS INCOMPLETE: There is effectively ZERO automated testing across both repos. LAB: `go test ./...` reports '[no test files]' for ALL 37 packages — not a single *_test.go exists anywhere (verified via find), despite the Makefile shipping `test` and `test-coverage` targets (Makefile:42-49) that have nothing to run. Untested critical packages include internal/api/handlers (36 files, ~381 handler methods), internal/api/middleware (auth), internal/domain/services (40 files incl. ai, desktop_security, digital_footprint), internal/infrastructure/database/repository, internal/detection/yara, internal/forensics (android+ios), and all internal/sources/* feed ingesters. The only 'test' artifact is cmd/test-safebrowsing/main.go, an ad-hoc manual binary, and cmd/worker/ is an empty directory (dead entrypoint). LAB's only CI (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) builds the Docker image and deploys straight to Azure Container Apps with no test/vet/lint gate. APP: there is NO test/ or integration_test/ directory at all; `flutter test` fails immediately with 'Test directory "test" not found.' even though flutter_test is declared in dev_dependencies (pubspec.yaml:45). So ~160 API-client methods, 19 security services, the ML classifiers (sms/url/behavior), realtime/WebSocket layer, and all providers have zero test coverage and no CI. `flutter analyze`: 768 total issues = 1 ERROR + 70 WARNINGS + 697 info. The 1 error is a non-exhaustive switch on ConnectivityResult (missing `satellite`) in lib/services/realtime/connection_manager.dart:229. Many of the 70 warnings are functional smells (unused _pegasusDomains/_knownMaliciousIPs/_scanNetwork etc., i.e. detection data declared but never consumed), which other domain auditors should cross-check. Bottom line: the claimed zero-defect/production-ready bar is unverifiable — there is no automated safety net in either repo.
